Scenario.title: CostaRicto Cyber Attack on Transportation Systems

overview.description: The CostaRicto campaign targets critical transportation infrastructure through a multi-stage attack involving reconnaissance, resource development, initial access via phishing and vulnerability exploitation, execution, persistence, privilege escalation, defense evasion, credential access, discovery, lateral movement, collection, exfiltration, and ultimately causing operational disruption.

threat_actor_profile.motivation: Disrupt transportation operations and potentially cause physical safety risks.
threat_actor_profile.skill_level: Highly skilled, state-sponsored attackers with advanced capabilities.

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: The attackers gather intelligence on the transportation system's architecture and identify potential entry points using OSINT collection techniques targeting domain information.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 673 Name: Developer Signing Maliciously Altered Software
stage.description: The attackers develop specialized malware tailored to exploit vulnerabilities in outdated transportation system components.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.description: Targeted phishing emails are crafted to exploit human error and gain initial access to the transportation network.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: Employees receive phishing emails containing malicious links or attachments, leading to initial compromise of the network.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 180 Name: Exploiting Incorrectly Configured Access Control Security Levels
stage.description: Unpatched IoT devices in the transportation network are exploited to gain unauthorized access.

stage.name: Execution
stage.surface: Web server services such as emails and databases
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 33 Name: HTTP Request Smuggling
stage.description: Custom backdoors are deployed to establish a foothold in the network, enabling further propagation.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 203 Name: Manipulate Registry Information
stage.description: Adversaries modify system configurations to ensure persistent access by creating registry keys for malicious processes.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 226 Name: Session Credential Falsification through Manipulation
stage.description: Elevated credentials are extracted to escalate privileges within the network.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: Security measures are disabled or evaded to prevent detection of malicious activity.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 568 Name: Capture Credentials via Keylogger
stage.description: Keyloggers are deployed to capture user credentials for unauthorized access.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 309 Name: Network Topology Mapping
stage.description: Adversaries scan the network to identify critical systems and data locations.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 325 Name: TCP Congestion Control Flag (ECN) Probe
stage.description: Moving between network segments using stolen credentials to propagate across the network.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 675 Name: Retrieve Data from Decommissioned Devices
stage.description: Sensitive operational data is collected from transportation systems for exfiltration.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 609 Name: Cellular Traffic Intercept
stage.description: Collected data is stealthily exfiltrated using encrypted channels to avoid detection.

stage.name: Impact
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 619 Name: Signal Strength Tracking
stage.description: Disruptions in traffic management systems are caused by manipulating traffic signals, leading to potential safety hazards and operational chaos.

Scenario.summary: The CostaRicto campaign successfully infiltrates a transportation system through a sophisticated multi-stage attack, leveraging both social engineering and technical exploitation to achieve its objectives. The attackers demonstrate advanced capabilities, from initial access through phishing and vulnerability exploitation to persistent internal movement, data collection, and ultimately impactful sabotage of critical infrastructure.
